retrieved_docs.title: Poland Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Poland syndrome affects about 1 in 36,000 to 50,000 newborns, with males more likely to be symptomatic than females. [9] Furthermore, right-sided predominance occurs more frequently in men than women. [10] Cases are usually sporadic, with rare familial cases described in the literature. [11] Given the likelihood that Poland syndrome is often underdiagnosed and under-reported, the exact incidence is not definitively known, and incidence rates are usually estimated. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Kearns-Sayre Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: KSS is very rare. While the exact prevalence of this condition is unknown, one study has reported a prevalence of 1.6 cases per 100,000 in the Finnish population.

retrieved_docs.title: Borrelia Burgdorferi -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Post-Lyme disease syndrome and chronic Lyme disease are areas of uncertainty due to fluctuating definitions and lack of research on incidence, prevalence, and pathogenesis. Post-Lyme disease syndrome is tentatively defined by the Infectious Disease Society of America (IDSA) as “a term to describe patients who have had well-documented Lyme disease and who remain symptomatic for many months to years after completion of appropriate antibiotic therapy.” IDSA’s recommendations have inclusion criteria to elucidate this syndrome further. These criteria include the onset of any of the following symptoms within 6 months of definitively diagnosed Lyme disease and persistence of symptoms for 6 months after completion of appropriate antibiotic therapy: Fatigue Widespread musculoskeletal pain Complaints of cognitive difficulties

retrieved_docs.title: Cornelia de Lange Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Because in individuals with CdLS the clinical presentation can vary widely, the syndrome can closely resemble other genetic disorders. Therefore genetic testing is recommended to diagnose the syndrome definitively and to rule out other genetic disorders, especially in non-classical CdLS phenotypes. [4] Autism spectrum disorder Rett syndrome [12] Roberts syndrome Nicolaides-Baraister syndrome DiGeorge syndrome [13] [14] [13] Deletions of chromosome 2q31 Partial duplication of 3q Fryns syndrome CHOPS syndrome Bohring-Opitz syndrome TAF6 or TAF1

retrieved_docs.title: Kearns-Sayre Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Neuroimaging cannot be used in isolation as a diagnostic modality since the findings are neither sensitive or specific enough to be considered part of the diagnostic criteria. However, as some patients develop a leukoencephalopathy, an MRI of the brain should be performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Westphal Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Westphal’s sign refers to a decreased or absent patellar tendon reflex, also known as the knee-jerk reflex, observed on physical exam. The patellar tendon reflex refers to a kicking-like motion produced by the extension of the knee joint upon the ipsilateral stimulation of the patellar tendon. The reflex is thought to play a role in maintaining balance. The realization of the importance of deep tendon reflexes in the physical exam is often attributed to Wilhelm Heinrich Erb and Carl Friedrich Otto Westphal, who both noted this finding in the late 1800s. [1] [2] Westphal’s sign thus receives its name from Carl Friedrich Otto Westphal. The patellar tendon reflex is a deep tendon reflex, similar to the biceps, brachioradialis, triceps, Achilles, and jaw jerk reflexes. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Keshan Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Integrating study findings emphasizes the diagnostic value of ECG in detecting latent Keshan disease and predicting disease progression. Furthermore, the correlation between familial history and advanced cardiac dysfunction provides insights into the genetic basis and severe cardiac implications of chronic Keshan disease. Despite the absence of a definitive diagnostic criterion, a comprehensive evaluation encompassing ECG assessments, familial history analysis, echocardiographic studies, and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) proves invaluable in excluding the other causes of cardiomyopathy, as well as guiding the diagnosis and establishing the prognosis of Keshan disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Beevor Sign -- Definition/Introduction -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pompe disease or type 2 glycogen storage disease Glucosamine (UDP-N-acetyl)-2-epimerase/N-acetylmannosamine kinase  (GNE) myopathy, an autosomal recessive disorder. One study reported this sign in 90% of patients with GNE. [10] Tubular aggregate myopathy Myotonic dystrophy Sporadic inclusion body myositis Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis [11] Acid maltase deficiency in an adult patient [12] Autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy type 2G, a telethoninopathy [13] FLAD1 -related lipid storage myopathy [14] Limb-girdle dysferlinopathy with a heterozygous missense variation in exon 10 of the DYSF gene [15] COL6A3 c.6817-2(IVS27)A>G variant associated with Bethlem myopathy [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Kluver-Bucy Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Human KBS is more or less restricted to case series and reports. Hence the exact prevalence is difficult to estimate.

retrieved_docs.title: Landau-Kleffner Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: It is difficult to express an estimate of the prevalence and incidence of the syndrome. Landau-Kleffner syndrome, indeed, is described as sporadic or limited case series. From the description of the syndrome, in 1957, no more than a few hundred cases have been reported in the literature. An epidemiologic study demonstrated that the incidence of children with LKS in Japan was about 1 in a million. [7] Furthermore, it emerges that males are more affected than females and that the reference age is between 3 and 8 years. However, documented cases also exist of younger children or adolescents with the syndrome. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Charles Bonnet Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: CB-type visual hallucinations without a visual loss should be labeled as separate entities. Cognitive decline is another contributory factor but is rarely the primary etiological factor. The most widely accepted definition of CBS is sensory deprivation-deafferentation- of the cerebral cortex and release phenomenon of hallucinations. CBS may mimic Alzheimer and Parkinsonism as cognition is impaired in all these conditions. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Gout -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Epidemiological estimates depend on the definition of the disease. A definitive diagnosis of gout is accepted in the presence of monosodium urate monohydrate crystals in the joint fluid or the identification of tophus. However, given the impracticality of identifying gout through these criteria alone, various case definitions have been devised, including self-reports, the Rome criteria, the New York criteria, the American College of Rheumatology (ACR) criteria, and the 2015 ACR/European League Against Rheumatism (EULAR) criteria. The 2015 ACR/EULAR criteria have a sensitivity of 92% and specificity of 89%, surpassing the accuracy of all previous definitions and ensuring a more precise and reliable diagnosis of gout in epidemiological studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Kearns-Sayre Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: KSS is defined by the following triad: onset before the age of 20, CPEO, and pigmentary retinopathy. Affected individuals have at least 1 of the following conditions: complete heart block, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) protein of more than 100 mg/dL, cerebellar ataxia, short stature, deafness, dementia, and endocrine abnormalities. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Seizure -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Epileptic syndromes serve to condense clinical information into useful nomenclature. Localization-related is used in this terminology to indicate seizures that arise from pathology in a localizable brain area. Idiopathic epilepsy is associated with no symptoms other than seizures. In symptomatic epilepsy, seizures reflect underlying identifiable brain disease. Cryptogenic refers to seizure disorders suspected to be symptomatic of underlying brain disease but are without definitive proof of the underlying cause. Specialists usually diagnose an epileptic syndrome. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: KS has an estimated frequency of about 1 in 32,000 to 1 in 86,000 individuals. [3] Although this condition was first described in Japan in the 1980s and was initially thought to be a syndrome specific to the East Asian population, recent data does not support an increased prevalence of this syndrome in any particular ethnicity. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Brudzinski Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial meningitis affects more than 1 million people per year globally and ranks as one of the top ten most deadly infectious diseases. [1] Bacterial meningitis has a high frequency of neurologic sequelae and mortality and is treatable with antibiotics, which makes the diagnosis extremely important and time-sensitive. As compared with other febrile illnesses, bacterial meningitis is relatively rare. Because of this, many clinical tests have been described to help providers differentiate meningitis from other causes of fever and headache. Two of the most commonly used are Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs. The original report of Brudzinski's sign was in the late 1800s by Dr. Josef Brudzinski. At that time, he described four maneuvers to aid in the clinical diagnosis of meningitis. The four maneuvers he described included the Brudzinski's reflex, the cheek sign, the nape of the neck sign and the symphyseal sign. [2] The most popular of the maneuvers is the nape of the neck sign, currently known as Brudzinski's sign.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal condition referred to as اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة is classified under the disorder خديج, and خديج is clinically associated with مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). خديج arises as a consequence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary maturation disorder syndrome). In patients with مرض السكري, severe complications such as بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation) are also clinically associated, reflecting the disease’s impact on peripheral vascular health. Specific forms of transient neonatal diabetes—including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes—are each mapped both to اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة and to مرض السكري, indicating that these entities represent neonatal presentations of diabetes that share features of a broader neonatal disorder category. Pharmacologically, norgestrel is contraindicated in individuals with مرض السكري, underscoring the need for careful medication selection in diabetic patients. Metabolic relationships are further highlighted by the link between متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) and مرض السكري, where metabolic syndrome is described as having مرض السكري as a child condition, reflecting diabetes as a component of the syndrome’s clinical spectrum. The term komorbidita is associated with both مرض السكري and متلازمة الأيض through a GDC (global disease classification) value, and an entity labeled Unknown is noted to have a GDC value of komorbidita, suggesting an undefined condition that shares comorbid characteristics with diabetes and metabolic syndrome. Collectively, these connections illustrate how neonatal disorders, specific forms of transient neonatal diabetes, adult diabetes complications, metabolic syndrome, and related pharmacologic considerations are interwoven within a comprehensive framework of disease classification and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1134
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is linked to several clinical and genetic contexts. It is clinically associated with toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and retinal disorders (اضطراب في الشبكية), reflecting the severe vascular complications that can arise in uncontrolled disease. Specific forms of neonatal diabetes, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and 2), as well as the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to the broader category of diabetes mellitus, indicating that these early‑onset variants are considered subtypes of the same metabolic disorder. Hormonal contraceptives desogestrel and norgestrel are contraindicated in patients with diabetes, underscoring the need for careful medication selection in this population. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is recorded as having diabetes as a child condition, illustrating that diabetes is a core component of the syndrome’s cluster of risk factors. The comorbidity concept (komorbidita) is connected to both diabetes and metabolic syndrome, highlighting the frequent co‑occurrence of diabetes with other metabolic and cardiovascular conditions. An additional, unspecified entity is noted to have a comorbidity value of komorbidita, though the exact nature of this relationship remains unclear. Together, these relationships emphasize the multifaceted nature of diabetes, its complications, its genetic forms, medication considerations, and its integral role within broader metabolic disease clusters.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” is linked to the concept of “komplikace” and to the Arabic word “حَصاة,” indicating an association between this qualifier and a stone‑related complication. In addition, “Allowed qualifier” is connected to “conditions associated with dizziness or vertigo,” which are also classified as “komplikace,” underscoring its relevance to vestibular‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 407
retrieved_docs.content: Diseases and injuries represent a broad medical category that includes genitourinary disorders (اضطراب بولي تناسلي) and also subsumes signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within this group, the entity “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is closely related to the general concept of Symptoms, which are considered synonymous or overlapping. A specific subset of these ill‑defined manifestations is malaise and fatigue, which is classified under signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Malaise and fatigue is mapped to the Arabic terms الشعور بالضعف (feeling of weakness) and النوام (nausea), and is additionally linked to الضعف العصبي (neurological weakness), reflecting its multifaceted clinical presentation. Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is identified as a type of فقر الدم (anemia). Anemia is clinically associated both with الشعور بالضعف (weakness) and with متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم (hypokalemia syndrome), indicating that patients with anemia often experience generalized weakness and may develop concurrent potassium deficiency. Thus, the weakness described by malaise and fatigue can be understood as a common clinical feature of anemia, linking the broader categories of diseases and injuries, ill‑defined symptom complexes, and specific hematologic disorders in a unified framework for patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2681
retrieved_docs.content: The term “komplikace” (complication) is described as being able to be qualified by specific conditions, and the descriptors “Allowed qualifier” and “can be qualified by.” indicate that certain entities are permissible qualifiers for this category. In particular, the complication category can be qualified by diabetic kidney disease (اعتلال الكلية السكري), by other complications of pregnancy, and by death (التموت). This framing underscores that diabetic kidney disease, pregnancy‑related complications, and mortality are recognized as valid examples of medical complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 5 -- Community 2958
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is connected to conditions associated with dizziness or vertigo and is also linked to the broader concept of komplikace. Within this framework of komplikace, specific complications such as انسداد الحالب (ureteral obstruction) and الالتصاق (adhesion) are included. This relationship highlights that the allowed qualifier pertains both to vestibular‑related symptoms and to particular pathological outcomes represented by komplikace, encompassing ureteral blockage and tissue adhesion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is identified as a medical condition whose broader parent term is the Arabic phrase اِضطِراب الرِّئَة, indicating that this Arabic term encompasses the concept of respiratory distress. Additionally, respiratory distress syndrome is cross‑referenced with the Arabic designation متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة, suggesting that both Arabic terms are synonymous or closely related descriptors for the same syndrome. This linkage clarifies the terminology used in multilingual clinical contexts and aids in consistent identification and communication of the condition across language barriers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 998
retrieved_docs.content: Malaise and fatigue, which is mapped to the Arabic expressions الشعور بالضعف and النوام and also associated with الضعف العصبي, serves as a parent category for the broader grouping termed signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions. Within this grouping, the concept غشي is identified as a child element. Additionally, the term Symptoms is described as related and possibly synonymous with signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions, indicating overlap in clinical description. Understanding these hierarchical and synonymous relationships helps clinicians recognize that malaise and fatigue and its Arabic equivalents encompass a range of nonspecific signs, including غشي, and that the label Symptoms may be used interchangeably with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb is associated with the morphology termed النَخَر and is linked to the broader category of lower‑extremity amputation, indicating that stump necrosis is a possible postoperative complication in diabetic patients. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is identified as a child concept of diabetes mellitus, and both diabetes mellitus and metabolic syndrome share a comorbidity value labeled “komorbidita,” suggesting that they frequently coexist and contribute to a compounded disease burden. An unspecified entity (“Unknown”) also carries the same comorbidity value, reinforcing the notion that “komorbidita” represents a broader category of co‑occurring conditions. In summary, diabetes mellitus serves as a central node connecting neonatal diabetes disorders, pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance, medication contraindications, retinal and peripheral vascular complications—including various levels of lower‑extremity amputation and stump

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 506
retrieved_docs.content: Signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions is a medical concept that groups together vague and nonspecific clinical presentations. It is closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the broader term Symptoms. Within this ill‑defined symptom category, the specific manifestation غشي is identified as a child concept, and malaise and fatigue is also classified as a constituent sign. Malaise and fatigue is further linked to several Arabic descriptors, being mapped to الشعور بالضعف and النوام, and additionally associated with الضعف العصبي. Hierarchically, signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions resides within the larger domain of diseases and injuries, indicating that these nonspecific signs are part of the overall disease and injury taxonomy. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians understand that vague symptoms such as malaise, fatigue, and غشي belong to an ill‑defined symptom cluster, guiding appropriate assessment and management within the broader context of disease classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 571
retrieved_docs.content: stump of the right lower limb is associated with the morphology “النَخَر” and is directly associated with the amputation procedure, indicating that stump necrosis is a recognized postoperative risk. Therapeutically, the drug ethynodioldiacetát is listed as contraindicated in patients with diabetes, emphasizing the need for medication safety considerations in this population. A GDC (global disease classification) value termed “komorbidita” is linked both to diabetes and to metabolic syndrome, and an unspecified “Unknown” entity also carries this GDC value, suggesting a broader categorization of comorbidity that encompasses diabetes‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 808
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central clinical entity that links a wide array of diagnoses, metabolic conditions, complications, screening results, and medication considerations. It functions as the parent of the “Diagnosis/Diseases Component” and has metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) as a child; both are assigned the GDC value “komorbidita,” indicating a comorbid relationship, and an additional unspecified entity shares this value. Several neonatal metabolic disorders—DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder); Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1; and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes—are explicitly mapped to diabetes, highlighting its relevance in early‑life disease phenotypes. Vascular and ocular sequelae of diabetes include retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية), toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم), and below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة). These amputation procedures are subsumed under the broader surgical category “amputation of lower
